summary: Jeff Atwood revisits his earlier criticism of 7-ZIP compression software after discovering its performance has dramatically improved. He conducts a benchmark comparing WinRAR and different 7-ZIP versions, finding that 7-ZIP 4.44 now matches WinRAR's speed while producing smaller archives. Atwood acknowledges his previous dismissal was based on outdated information and publicly corrects his earlier recommendation. While he still prefers WinRAR's shell integration, he concedes 7-ZIP is now a viable competitor.
key_points: 7-ZIP performance doubled over two years, matching WinRAR's compression speed
7-ZIP produces slightly smaller archives than WinRAR (125 MB vs 135 MB in his test)
The author acknowledges the limitation of his single-machine benchmark but finds the results compelling
WinRAR still has advantages in shell integration and user experience
Open source software can catch up to commercial alternatives through sustained development
money_quotes: 7-ZIP performance has doubled over the last two years.
Performance is no longer a reason to choose WinRAR over 7-ZIP.
Granted, this is a sample size of one, a single test on a single machine, but it's hard to ignore the dramatic reversal of fortune.
key_insight: Software recommendations require periodic reassessment as open source projects can dramatically improve over time, sometimes surpassing commercial alternatives.
